caveats: a: The occupational source covers all foundries at NAICS 3315.
a: Occupations contain both automatable information tasks and non-automatable physical or accountable tasks.
rho: No 331513-specific longitudinal AI adoption study was located.
rho: Some benefits may appear as yield or throughput rather than labor savings.
e: Eligibility is inferred from industry structure rather than observed transactions.
e: Environmental and product-liability diligence can disqualify otherwise viable firms.
s5: The source covers all U.S. employer businesses, not metalcasters.
s5: Stated succession intentions are not completed sale probabilities.
q: No public 331513 dataset directly measures retention of AI-created surplus.
q: Capture varies sharply between qualified niche castings and more substitutable work.
d5: The BLS projection is for all foundries and a ten-year horizon.
d5: Customer mix can dominate the sector average for an individual foundry.
o: The small residual below one allows rare outsourced or nontraditional operating arrangements.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue is earned from engineered castings priced by part, pound, lot, or program, often with separate pattern or tooling charges and optional heat treatment, finishing, testing, and machining. Repeat qualified programs and steel or energy surcharge mechanisms are common economic features.
lens.deviation_from_default: The default control-acquisition lens is narrowed to independent contract foundries because captive plants are not normally standalone acquisition targets and have materially different customer, transfer-pricing, and availability economics.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive OEM foundries, steel investment foundries, integrated steel mills, manufacturers whose principal product is a finished assembly rather than a casting, and passive minority interests or real-estate-only shells without operating control.
lens.included_activities: Independent repeat-contract foundries producing carbon, low-alloy, and high-alloy steel castings by non-investment processes for external industrial, mining, rail, power, transportation, and defense customers.

a.bridge: BLS reports the occupational mix for four-digit NAICS 3315 Foundries rather than 331513. Production occupations are 66.92%, installation/maintenance/repair 7.33%, and office/administrative support 5.25%; the estimate maps the information-heavy portions of management, engineering, sales, office, planning, estimating, quality documentation, and maintenance diagnostics into the AI-exposed share. Because steel foundries can be more engineered and lower-volume than the all-foundry average, the proxy may understate exposure, while counting whole occupational groups would overstate task-level exposure.
caveats: The occupational source covers all foundries at NAICS 3315.
Occupations contain both automatable information tasks and non-automatable physical or accountable tasks.
a.change_evidence: A 331513-specific task inventory with hours by occupation, workflow-level AI feasibility, and current software adoption would materially narrow the range.
a.rationale: Most direct melting, molding, pouring, shakeout, grinding, material handling, and inspection work remains physical. AI exposure is concentrated in quoting, casting and gating design support, production scheduling, purchasing, maintenance analysis, quality records, customer service, and administrative workflows.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for four-digit NAICS 3315 Foundries from $26.9 billion in 2024 to $24.8 billion in 2034, a -0.8% annual rate. Applying that rate for five years gives approximately 0.96. The range is widened because 331513 serves a different mix from all foundries: machinery, engines and power transmission, aerospace, shipbuilding, rail, mining, and other industrial markets have differing positive long-run output projections and pronounced cycles. The broader-foundry proxy may understate a steel foundry concentrated in durable qualified niches or overstate one exposed to substitution and weak capital-goods cycles.
caveats: The BLS projection is for all foundries and a ten-year horizon.
Customer mix can dominate the sector average for an individual foundry.
d5.change_evidence: 331513-specific real shipments, backlog, capacity utilization, import penetration, program wins and losses, customer end-market forecasts, and substitution rates would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: The base reflects mild secular contraction rather than disappearance. Large, strong, reliable steel castings retain roles across machinery, transportation, rail, mining, cement, power, and aerospace, but substitution, offshoring, customer insourcing, and cyclical capital spending create a wide outcome range.

caveats: Eligibility is inferred from industry structure rather than observed transactions.
Environmental and product-liability diligence can disqualify otherwise viable firms.
e.change_evidence: A census of 331513 establishments linked to ownership, captive status, revenue, EBITDA, customer concentration, environmental history, and management depth would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: Independent contract foundries fit a control-buyout lens when they have diversified repeat programs, transferable customer qualifications, credible environmental controls, and a capable second management layer. Captive operations, customer concentration, volatile earnings, heavy maintenance capital, environmental liabilities, and owner-dependent technical relationships reduce the eligible fraction.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 331513 ($815,672,368) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 331513 ($3,637,317,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $370,248 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 331513); class <5: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $734,591 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $144,127 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 4 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,334,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $261,829 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 16 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,744,438 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $930,859 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 46 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,419,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,848,106 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 27 firms, avg revenue/firm $35,482,630 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,961,692 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 16 firms, avg revenue/firm $134,286,188 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $26,346,950 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 73

caveats: The small residual below one allows rare outsourced or nontraditional operating arrangements.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of commercially scaled lights-out steel casting, outsourced asset-light operating models, or widespread virtualized production with no controlled plant would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Purchased scrap and alloys must be received and melted; molds and cores must be produced; molten steel must be poured; and castings must be cleaned, finished, tested, and shipped. Even with automation and AI, a physical operating entity with safety, quality, maintenance, and environmental accountability is almost always required.

caveats: No public 331513 dataset directly measures retention of AI-created surplus.
Capture varies sharply between qualified niche castings and more substitutable work.
q.change_evidence: Program-level contract terms, renewal and rebid outcomes, realized price changes, customer concentration, switching rates, and gross-margin retention following measurable productivity improvements would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Approved patterns, casting know-how, qualification records, low-volume large parts, and the cost and risk of changing suppliers can support retention and permit sharing some productivity gains. Capture is limited by sophisticated OEM purchasing, annual productivity demands, competitive rebids, raw-material surcharge transparency, customer concentration, and the possibility that improvements are passed through as price or delivery performance.

caveats: No 331513-specific longitudinal AI adoption study was located.
Some benefits may appear as yield or throughput rather than labor savings.
rho.change_evidence: Foundry-level pilots reporting labor hours, scrap, rework, yield, quotation time, downtime, and implementation cost before and after AI deployment would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Realization should be meaningful in estimating, scheduling, design assistance, procurement, quality analysis, documentation, and predictive maintenance, but legacy systems, variable low-volume work, safety-critical decisions, customer approvals, metallurgical expertise, data quality, and environmental compliance constrain conversion of technical exposure into economic benefit.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports that 52.3% of U.S. employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older and that 22% of employer-business owners planned a sale or transfer within five years. The base uses the five-year intention rate as a cross-industry starting point and discounts the implication for steel foundries because intentions do not always become completed control sales and permitting, customer qualification, capital needs, family succession, or strategic-buyer competition can prevent an LMM acquisition. The proxy may understate pressure in an older industrial niche but likely overstates completed independent transactions.
caveats: The source covers all U.S. employer businesses, not metalcasters.
Stated succession intentions are not completed sale probabilities.
s5.change_evidence: 331513 owner-age microdata, broker listings, closed-control transactions, plant closures, and family or employee succession outcomes over five years would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Aging ownership creates credible succession supply, while the limited pool of qualified buyers and the operational, regulatory, and capital intensity of steel foundries restrain realized sale availability.

principal_driver: Sticky qualified programs for difficult steel castings, combined with practical AI gains in engineering and operating information flows, support retention and improvement without assuming physical labor displacement.
principal_weakness: The eligible and actually available target pool is narrowed by heavy physical assets, environmental and safety exposure, cyclical customer demand, concentrated programs, and the prevalence of captive or owner-dependent operations.

sources.supports: Four-digit foundry occupational employment and shares used to bridge AI task exposure.
